original_abstract: The structure and configuration of reaction centers, which dominantly govern the catalytic behaviors, often undergo dynamic transformations under reaction conditions, yet little is known about how to exploit these features to favor the catalytic functions. Here, we demonstrate a facile light activation strategy over a TiO<sub>2</sub>-supported Cu catalyst to regulate the dynamic restructuring of Cu active sites during low-temperature methanol steam reforming. Under illumination, the thermally deactivated Cu/TiO<sub>2</sub> undergoes structural restoration from inoperative Cu<sub>2</sub>O to the originally active metallic Cu caused by photoexcited charge carriers from TiO<sub>2</sub>, thereby leading to substantially enhanced activity and stability. Given the low-intensity solar irradiation, the optimized Cu/TiO<sub>2</sub> displays a H<sub>2</sub> production rate of 1724.1 μmol g<sup>-1</sup> min<sup>-1</sup>, outperforming most of the conventional photocatalytic and thermocatalytic processes. Taking advantages of the strong light-matter-reactant interaction, we achieve in situ manipulation of the Cu active sites, suggesting the feasibility for real-time functionalization of catalysts.
humanized_abstract: The dynamic structural changes of catalysts and their correlation with catalysis are poorly understood. In this study, we show that the light activation over a TiO2-supported Cu catalyst can control the dynamic structural changes of Cu active sites during low-temperature methanol steam reforming. Under the light activation, the thermally deactivated Cu active sites of TiO2-supported Cu catalyst can be restored from inactive Cu2O to the active metallic Cu by photoinduced charge carriers generated from TiO2. As a result, the light activated Cu/TiO2 catalyst presents a higher catalytic performance. With low intensity solar light, the optimized Cu/TiO2 catalyst shows a high H2 production rate of 1724.1 μmol g-1 min-1, which is higher than most of the reported photocatalytic and thermocatalytic processes. The in situ adjustment of Cu active sites by strong light-matter-reactant interaction reveals the possibility for real-time functionalization of catalysts.

With low intensity solar light, the optimized Cu/TiO2 catalyst shows a high H2 production rate of 1724.1 μmol g-1 min-1, which is higher than most of the reported photocatalytic and thermocatalytic processes. The in situ adjustment of Cu active sites by strong light-matter-reactant interaction reveals the possibility for real-time functionalization of catalysts.
